key_points: Force is an interaction that changes the velocity, direction or shape of an object
Balanced forces maintain equilibrium and do not produce acceleration
Unbalanced forces cause acceleration according to Newton’s Second Law
Inertia is directly proportional to mass and represents resistance to motion change
Newton’s First Law explains why objects maintain their state of rest or uniform motion
Momentum is a vector quantity equal to the product of mass and velocity
Newton’s Second Law states that force equals rate of change of momentum
Mathematically Newton’s Second Law is expressed as F = ma
The law of conservation of momentum states that total momentum remains constant in the absence of external forces
Newton’s Third Law states that action and reaction forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction
examples: A stationary object remains at rest due to balanced forces
A moving car stops gradually due to frictional force
Passengers in a turning bus lean outward due to inertia of direction
Recoil of a gun when a bullet is fired demonstrates action–reaction forces
Rocket engines expel gases downward causing upward motion
Two skaters pushing each other illustrate conservation of momentum
formulas: Conservation of Momentum → m1u1 + m2u2 = m1v1 + m2v2
exam_tips: Understand derivation of F = ma from rate of change of momentum
Remember that momentum and force are vector quantities
Inertia depends only on mass, not velocity
Action and reaction forces always act on different bodies
Practice numerical problems involving force, momentum and acceleration
